title: Low BMI in a Young Adult Woman: A Multisystem Workup Refinement
subtitle: A Resident Training Module: recurrent chest infections + BMI 15–16 + amenorrhea + micronutrient deficiencies with normal stool frequency — immunodeficiency and atypical CF, TB/IGRA, celiac with total IgA, occult malabsorption, FHA vs organic causes, eating-disorder screening, and the sequenced Indian-context algorithm
authors: Resident training module — illustrative teaching vignette

relatedChapters.title: Unexplained Weight Loss in Older Adults

patient.chiefComplaint: Chronic low weight (BMI ~15–16), irregular menstrual cycles, recurrent chest infections since childhood, and lower abdominal pain
pmh: Iron deficiency, vitamin B12 deficiency, vitamin D deficiency
TSH 5.74 mIU/L on levothyroxine 25 mcg; repeat TSH 1.54 — LT4 being stopped
Master's degree in Clinical Psychology — high health literacy
medications: Levothyroxine 25 mcg daily — being discontinued pending repeat thyroid panel

caseDescription: A 24-year-old woman with BMI ~15–16 presents with lifelong recurrent chest infections, irregular menses, micronutrient deficiencies (iron, B12, vitamin D), normal stool frequency, and lower abdominal pain. She was on levothyroxine 25 mcg for TSH 5.74; repeat TSH 1.54 — stopping LT4 is appropriate. Low BMI in a young adult is a final common pathway for endocrine, immunologic, infectious, nutritional, and behavioural causes. This module refines the workup for the Indian context: HRCT and sweat chloride (atypical CF), IGRA and sputum CBNAAT (TB), tTG-IgA with total serum IgA (celiac), occult malabsorption stool studies despite normal bowel habit, immunoglobulins and lymphocyte subsets, reproductive axis (FHA vs organic), adrenal screen, DEXA, and structured eating-disorder screening (SCOFF/EDE-Q) plus ECG. Sequenced algorithm and key pitfalls included.
keyFindings: BMI 15–16 + amenorrhea + micronutrient deficiencies — do not anchor on subclinical hypothyroidism alone
Recurrent chest infections since childhood — CXR insufficient; consider PID, atypical CF, TB
Normal stool frequency does not exclude celiac, giardiasis, ileal Crohn's, or pancreatic-sufficient CF
Always co-order total IgA with tTG-IgA
Eating disorder (AN/ARFID) is the most statistically likely unifying diagnosis if organic workup negative
finalDiagnosis: Pending completed workup. Leading unifying possibilities after organic exclusion: functional hypothalamic amenorrhea from chronic energy deficit ± restrictive eating pattern (anorexia nervosa or ARFID). Management then pivots to nutritional rehabilitation, psychological support, and bone protection (DEXA-guided) rather than unnecessary endocrine replacement. If TB, celiac, immunodeficiency, CF, or adrenal insufficiency is found — treat the specific cause first.
learningObjectives: Structure a sequenced multisystem workup for severe underweight in a young woman
Apply Indian-context additions: HRCT, IGRA/CBNAAT, sweat chloride, total IgA with celiac serology
Recognise FHA pattern versus organic hypothalamic-pituitary-ovarian disease
Avoid pitfalls: IgA deficiency, normal stools, dismissed childhood infections, missed eating disorders
Know when to stop unnecessary levothyroxine and recheck thyroid axis after 6–8 weeks

items.title: Why low BMI in a young adult demands a multisystem differential
items.content: Severe underweight (BMI ~15–16) in a 24-year-old woman is not a single-diagnosis problem — it is a final common pathway for inadequate intake, malabsorption, hypermetabolism, chronic infection, immunodeficiency, endocrine disease, autoimmune disorders, and disordered eating. This patient adds recurrent chest infections since childhood, amenorrhea, iron/B12/vitamin D deficiency, normal stool frequency, and brief levothyroxine exposure. Each clue opens a branch; premature anchoring on subclinical hypothyroidism or 'childhood asthma' misses treatable causes (TB, celiac, CVID, atypical CF) and the commonest unifying diagnosis — functional hypothalamic amenorrhea with energy deficit, often from restrictive eating.
keyPoints: Indian context: TB, celiac, PID, atypical CF.

items.title: Why HRCT, sweat chloride, and TB screening are mandatory with childhood chest infections
items.content: Recurrent chest infections since childhood plus low BMI is a red flag for primary immunodeficiency or atypical cystic fibrosis — CXR alone is insufficient. Add HRCT chest for bronchiectasis, tree-in-bud pattern, subtle structural disease. Sweat chloride test: atypical CF can present in adulthood with recurrent infections, low BMI, and normal stool frequency (pancreatic-sufficient variants). In India: sputum AFB/CBNAAT and IGRA (Quantiferon-TB Gold) to rule out pulmonary and latent TB definitively. PFTs (spirometry + DLCO) for baseline and bronchodilator response. If serum immunoglobulins are normal but suspicion remains: lymphocyte subsets (CD4, CD8, CD19/CD20), IgG subclasses, and vaccine response titers (anti-tetanus, anti-pneumococcal) for functional antibody deficiency (CVID pattern).
keyPoints: IGRA + CBNAAT; Ig subsets if IgG normal.

items.title: How to evaluate occult malabsorption when stool frequency is normal
items.content: Isolated Fe, B12, and vitamin D deficiency with low BMI but normal bowel habit strongly suggests occult malabsorption or inadequate intake. Celiac: tTG-IgA plus total serum IgA simultaneously — if IgA deficient, tTG-IgA is falsely negative; use tTG-IgG or DGP-IgG. HLA DQ2/DQ8 if serology equivocal (high negative predictive value). Stool routine and microscopy plus ova and parasites ×3 — giardiasis, strongyloidiasis, hookworm cause insidious malabsorption without diarrhoea. Stool Sudan stain or fecal fat — occult fat malabsorption in pancreatic insufficiency, CF, or celiac. Fecal calprotectin for Crohn's ileitis (can cause B12 deficiency and low weight with normal stool pattern). Do not let normal stool frequency provide false reassurance.

items.title: Why stopping levothyroxine is correct when TSH normalised and malnutrition is present
items.content: TSH was 5.74 on levothyroxine 25 mcg; repeat TSH 1.54. In a malnourished low-BMI woman, mild TSH elevation is often functional — euthyroid sick syndrome, low-T3 state, or transient subclinical hypothyroidism — not true hypothyroidism requiring lifelong LT4. Stopping LT4 and rechecking TSH, free T4, and TPO antibodies in 6–8 weeks is appropriate. Do not anchor on subclinical hypothyroidism as the unifying diagnosis for low weight, amenorrhea, and micronutrient deficiencies. TPO antibodies confirm autoimmune thyroid disease if hypothyroidism persists off LT4.

items.title: How to evaluate FHA versus organic causes of amenorrhea in severe underweight
items.content: Irregular cycles + BMI 15–16 + chronic low weight is classic for functional hypothalamic amenorrhea (FHA) from energy deficit — but organic causes must be excluded first. Labs: FSH, LH, estradiol, prolactin. Low gonadotropins + low estradiol = FHA pattern when pituitary/prolactinoma excluded. Pelvic ultrasound for lower abdominal pain — rules out PCOS, ovarian cysts, TB involvement (peritoneal/ovarian). Total testosterone/DHEAS only if hirsutism/acne (lean PCOS vs FHA). AM cortisol + ACTH — adrenal insufficiency especially with autoimmune diathesis. IGF-1 ± GH stimulation if height/growth concern after malabsorption excluded. DEXA indicated with chronic low BMI + amenorrhea + vitamin D deficiency.
keyPoints: FHA: low LH/FSH + low E2 after exclusion.
Pelvic USG for pain; prolactin mandatory.

items.title: How to build a comprehensive nutritional and inflammatory panel
items.content: CBC with peripheral smear, ESR, CRP — assess microcytic vs macrocytic cytopenias and occult inflammation. Serum albumin, prealbumin (reflects recent intake better than albumin), folate, zinc, selenium. Repeat iron studies, vitamin D, B12. HbA1c and fasting glucose — baseline metabolic screen. Bone mineral density (DEXA) with chronic low BMI, amenorrhea, and vitamin D deficiency — high osteopenia/osteoporosis risk. Chronic energy deficit often produces mixed micronutrient deficiencies simultaneously.

items.title: Why ANA and TPO antibodies fit the autoimmune diathesis screen
items.content: ANA with consideration of anti-dsDNA and ENA panel if ANA positive and connective tissue disease suspected. TPO antibodies essential — confirms whether LT4 was ever truly indicated and screens autoimmune thyroid disease if off LT4 TSH rises again. Autoimmune clustering (thyroid, celiac, adrenal) is common in malabsorption and underweight presentations. Adrenal insufficiency must be excluded with AM cortisol and ACTH before attributing fatigue and weight loss to behaviour alone.

items.title: Why eating-disorder screening is mandatory despite psychology training
items.content: Structured screening: SCOFF questionnaire, EDE-Q. High health literacy (Master's in Clinical Psychology) does not preclude anorexia nervosa or ARFID — education can paradoxically mask or rationalise restrictive behaviours. AN/ARFID can unify every finding: low BMI, micronutrient deficiencies, FHA, bradycardia/hypotension, and even recurrent chest infections (poor cough reflex, aspiration, malnutrition-related immunosuppression). ECG for QT prolongation and bradycardia in eating disorders with electrolyte risk. In low-BMI young women, disordered eating is statistically the most common unifying cause if organic workup is negative. Use sensitive framing given her professional background.
keyPoints: Most common unifying diagnosis if organic negative.

items.title: How to sequence the diagnostic algorithm in eight parallel steps
items.content: Step 1: Stop LT4 → recheck TSH, free T4, TPO-Ab in 6–8 weeks. Step 2: Parallel TB screen — HRCT chest + IGRA + sputum CBNAAT; if lymphadenopathy or peritoneal findings → USG/CT abdomen ± ascitic workup. Step 3: Parallel celiac/CF — tTG-IgA + total IgA + sweat chloride; if celiac serology negative but suspicion high → HLA DQ2/DQ8. Step 4: Immunodeficiency — IgG/A/M, IgG subclasses, lymphocyte subsets; vaccine titers if clinically documented infections despite normal IgG. Step 5: Reproductive/endocrine — FSH, LH, E2, prolactin, 8 AM cortisol + ACTH; pelvic USG. Step 6: Nutrition/metabolic — CBC, ESR/CRP, albumin, prealbumin, folate, zinc, iron, B12, Vit D, DEXA, HbA1c. Step 7: Stool — R&M ×3, O&P, calprotectin, Sudan/fecal fat. Step 8: Psychological — ECG + SCOFF/EDE-Q with sensitive framing.
keyPoints: Psychological screen last but not optional.

items.title: Why five common pitfalls must be avoided in this vignette
items.content: Pitfall 1: Missing IgA deficiency when ordering tTG-IgA — always co-order total IgA. Pitfall 2: Anchoring on subclinical hypothyroidism — TSH 5.74 in malnutrition is often functional; stopping LT4 is correct. Pitfall 3: Dismissing childhood chest infections as asthma — pursue PID, atypical CF, TB until excluded. Pitfall 4: Normal stool frequency providing false reassurance — fat, B12, and iron malabsorption occur with normal bowel habit. Pitfall 5: Ignoring eating disorder because of psychology training — AN/ARFID remains the most likely unifier if organic workup is negative.

items.title: How management pivots when organic workup is negative
items.content: If TB, celiac, immunodeficiency, CF, adrenal insufficiency, and other organic causes are excluded, the unifying diagnosis is likely functional hypothalamic amenorrhea secondary to chronic energy deficit, potentially with underlying restrictive eating pattern (AN or ARFID). Management: nutritional rehabilitation (supervised refeeding), psychological support (specialist eating-disorder care), bone protection (calcium, vitamin D, DEXA-guided therapy), avoid unnecessary hormone replacement until weight restored. Monitor cardiac rhythm during refeeding. Treat specific organic diagnoses aggressively if found — they take precedence over behavioural attribution.
keyPoints: Treat organic cause if found first.

items.title: Why TB must be ruled out definitively in chronic low weight in India
items.content: Childhood-onset respiratory infections plus chronic low weight in the Indian epidemiologic context mandate rigorous TB exclusion: IGRA, sputum CBNAAT, HRCT chest. TB can involve lungs, lymph nodes, peritoneum, and ovaries — explaining abdominal pain, amenorrhea, and wasting without diarrhoea. If imaging shows lymphadenopathy or peritoneal disease, extend to abdominal USG/CT and fluid sampling as indicated. Do not attribute constitutional symptoms to underweight alone until TB is excluded.

items.question: Why must total serum IgA be ordered simultaneously with tTG-IgA in this patient?
items.answer: Because selective IgA deficiency is common and causes falsely negative tTG-IgA even when celiac disease is present. Without knowing total IgA, a negative tTG-IgA may be misinterpreted as excluding celiac when the test is simply invalid. If IgA is deficient, switch to tTG-IgG or DGP-IgG. This pitfall is especially important in patients with recurrent infections and malabsorption patterns.

items.question: How should the first-week workup be structured for this 24-year-old with BMI 15–16 and recurrent chest infections?
items.answer: Run parallel panels rather than delaying: (1) stop LT4 and schedule thyroid recheck at 6–8 weeks; (2) HRCT + IGRA + sputum CBNAAT; (3) tTG-IgA + total IgA + sweat chloride; (4) immunoglobulins and lymphocyte subsets; (5) FSH/LH/E2/prolactin, AM cortisol/ACTH, pelvic USG; (6) CBC, inflammatory markers, nutritional panel, DEXA referral; (7) stool studies ×3 with calprotectin and fecal fat; (8) ECG and SCOFF/EDE-Q. TB, celiac, CF, and immunodeficiency screens should not wait for nutritional results.

items.question: Why is eating-disorder screening essential even in a woman with a Master's degree in Clinical Psychology?
items.answer: Because health literacy and professional knowledge do not protect against anorexia nervosa or ARFID — and may enable rationalisation or concealment of restrictive eating. In young women with BMI 15–16, amenorrhea, and micronutrient deficiencies, disordered eating is statistically the most common unifying diagnosis once organic causes are excluded. Missing it delays life-saving nutritional rehabilitation and psychiatric care. Sensitive, non-judgmental screening (SCOFF, EDE-Q) is mandatory, not optional.

items.question: How do you distinguish functional hypothalamic amenorrhea from organic hypogonadotropic hypogonadism in this patient?
items.answer: After excluding prolactinoma (prolactin, pituitary MRI if indicated), adrenal insufficiency (AM cortisol, ACTH), and significant organic illness (TB, celiac, CF, immunodeficiency), FHA shows low LH/FSH and low estradiol in the setting of chronic energy deficit and low BMI. Organic HH would require structural pituitary disease or other pathology — MRI if prolactin elevated or red flags present. Pelvic ultrasound excludes ovarian pathology. FHA improves with weight restoration; premature oestrogen replacement without addressing energy deficit is inappropriate.

items.stem: When ordering tTG-IgA for suspected celiac disease in this patient, what must be co-ordered?
items.explanation: IgA deficiency causes falsely negative tTG-IgA — always order total IgA; if deficient, use tTG-IgG or DGP-IgG.

items.stem: A 24-year-old with recurrent chest infections since childhood, low BMI, and normal stool frequency should undergo:
options: HRCT chest and sweat chloride testing
items.explanation: Atypical CF with pancreatic sufficiency can present with normal stools — HRCT and sweat chloride are essential; CXR is insufficient.

items.stem: In the Indian context, which panel best rules out TB contributing to chronic low weight?
options: IGRA plus sputum CBNAAT plus HRCT chest
items.explanation: IGRA, CBNAAT, and HRCT provide definitive TB exclusion; CXR alone misses subtle and extrapulmonary disease.

items.stem: TSH was 5.74 on levothyroxine 25 mcg; repeat TSH 1.54 in a malnourished patient. Best next step?
options: Stop levothyroxine and recheck TSH, free T4, TPO-Ab in 6–8 weeks
items.explanation: Mild TSH elevation in malnutrition is often functional; stopping LT4 and reassessing avoids overtreatment.

items.stem: Low LH, low FSH, and low estradiol in a woman with BMI 15–16 after organic exclusion most likely indicate:
options: Functional hypothalamic amenorrhea from energy deficit
Primary ovarian insufficiency with high FSH
items.explanation: Classic FHA pattern with severe underweight — PCOS has different hormonal profile; POI shows high FSH.

items.stem: Normal stool frequency in this patient:
options: Does not exclude occult fat, B12, or iron malabsorption
items.explanation: Celiac, giardiasis, ileal Crohn's, and pancreatic-sufficient CF can malabsorb without diarrhoea.

items.stem: If serum IgG is normal but recurrent sinopulmonary infections are well documented, the next step is:
options: Lymphocyte subsets and/or vaccine response titers
items.explanation: CVID and specific antibody deficiency can present with normal total IgG — functional antibody testing clarifies.

items.stem: The most statistically likely unifying diagnosis if comprehensive organic workup is negative in this 24-year-old woman is:
options: Functional hypothalamic amenorrhea with restrictive eating pattern
items.explanation: BMI 15–16, amenorrhea, micronutrient deficiencies — FHA ± AN/ARFID after organic exclusion.

items.stem: DEXA scanning is indicated in this patient because of:
options: Chronic low BMI, amenorrhea, and vitamin D deficiency
items.explanation: Hypoestrogenism and malnutrition confer high osteopenia/osteoporosis risk — DEXA guides bone protection.

items.stem: Which stool tests address occult malabsorption despite normal bowel habit?
options: Three samples for ova and parasites plus Sudan stain/fecal fat and calprotectin
No stool tests if frequency normal
items.explanation: Giardiasis, fat malabsorption, and ileal inflammation can be detected without diarrhoea.

items.statement: A negative tTG-IgA always excludes celiac disease without checking total IgA.
items.explanation: False. Selective IgA deficiency causes false-negative tTG-IgA — total IgA must be measured.

items.statement: Cystic fibrosis always presents with steatorrhoea in adulthood.
items.explanation: False. Atypical pancreatic-sufficient CF variants can have normal stool frequency.

items.statement: A Master's degree in psychology precludes the need for eating-disorder screening.
items.explanation: False. High health literacy does not exclude AN/ARFID — screening remains mandatory.

items.statement: Stopping levothyroxine with planned 6–8 week recheck is appropriate when TSH has normalised in a malnourished patient on low-dose LT4.
items.explanation: True. Avoid anchoring on subclinical hypothyroidism — reassess off LT4 before committing to lifelong therapy.

items.statement: Childhood recurrent chest infections in this context can be dismissed as benign asthma without HRCT or immunodeficiency workup.
items.explanation: False. Major clue toward PID, atypical CF, or TB until excluded.

items.statement: IGRA and sputum CBNAAT are appropriate TB screening tests in chronic low weight in India.
items.explanation: True. Part of the parallel TB rule-out screen with HRCT.

items.assertion: Total serum IgA should be ordered with tTG-IgA in this underweight patient with micronutrient deficiencies.
items.reason: Selective IgA deficiency causes falsely negative tTG-IgA results, missing celiac disease.
items.explanation: Both true — reason explains assertion. Core pitfall in celiac screening.

items.assertion: Structured eating-disorder screening should be performed even if organic workup is still in progress.
items.reason: Anorexia nervosa and ARFID can explain low BMI, amenorrhea, and micronutrient deficiencies and may coexist with or mimic organic disease.
items.explanation: Both true — sensitive parallel screening does not require waiting for all organic results.
